result:
I keep thinking about the moment just before a story begins.

Not the opening sentence, not the first scene, but the hush that exists one heartbeat earlier—when the page is still blank, the room is still quiet, and everything is possible. It’s the same hush that lingers in a theater after the lights go down but before the curtain moves, when the darkness feels almost liquid, like you could cup it in your palms and drink.  

I go back to that hush again and again, the way some people return to the house they grew up in, standing on the sidewalk, afraid to knock. I suspect every creative act is born there: in the non-place where intention hasn’t yet hardened into form. A painter’s first brushstroke is already a betrayal of every other painting that might have been. A composer’s opening chord assassinates the silence it replaces. And yet we keep reaching, because the hush is intolerably fertile; it swells with ghosts that demand bodies.

Sometimes I wonder if that’s what we really mourn when we mourn our own childhoods: not the passage of time, but the evacuation of that ante-room. Children live inside the hush habitually. Watch a four-year-old turn a cardboard box into a spaceship: the transformation happens in the gap between eyeblink and breath, faster than language can pollute it. The box is never *just* a box; it is the glittering moment *before* the box decides what it will become. Then we learn nouns, and gravity sets in.

The cruelest trick of adulthood is how we trick ourselves out of that space. We schedule wonder, we monetize risk, we turn “I don’t know” into a problem instead of a country worth inhabiting. We build entire identities to avoid standing once more in the hush, because the hush is also where everything unfinished about us waits with its mouth open. Easier to answer emails, label shelves, argue online—any spell to keep from hearing the small click that announces the next sentence of our lives has arrived and is politely waiting to be spoken.

But the hush keeps sending postcards. It arrives at 3 a.m. when the ceiling pauses between passing headlights, or in the grocery aisle when you forget what you came for and suddenly remember being seven, clutching your mother’s hand, hypnotized by the bright maze of boxes. It slips into the pause your lover leaves after “We need to talk,” and for one merciful instant nothing has been ruined yet. It even hides in the split-second after the glass leaves your hand but before it shatters on the kitchen tile—an arrested galaxy of reflex and consequence.

I used to think the job of a writer was to fill the hush with sentences. Now I think the job is to build a door back into it, to make the reader homesick for a place they didn’t know they’d lost. Not by describing the hush—that would be like mailing someone a kiss—but by arranging words so that a faint reflection of it leaks through the cracks. You finish the last line of a story and, if you’re lucky, the silence that rises up afterward isn’t empty; it’s the same shape as the hush you started with, only now it’s inside the reader, turning like a slow planet.

Maybe that’s the closest we get to secular grace: creating artifacts that temporarily restore someone’s citizenship in possibility. We can’t live in the hush, not continuously; we’d never finish paying rent, never keep a promise, never catch a plane. But we can visit, the way astronomers visit remote mountaintops to look at stars they will never touch. We can carry a pocket-sized shred of it back into the lit world, a contraband vacuum that resists the pressure of agendas and identities. And when we meet another person who is also smuggling that invisible vacuum, we recognize each other instantly, even if we never speak of it. A certain gentleness in the eyes, a hesitation before replying, as if testing the weight of each word against a secret scale.

So if you ever find yourself in an elevator that stops between floors for no reason, or wake to snow falling at 5 a.m. when the city hasn’t yet remembered it owns horns and sirens—pause. Feel the hush pooling like cool water in your lungs. Nothing is required of you there; that’s the whole point. You are not a role, not a biography, not a five-year plan. You are the unwritten sentence, the unstruck match, the runway lights blinking for a plane that still considers whether it will take off. Stay as long as you can before the lights come up, the page fills, the box becomes merely a box again. The hush will let you go; it always does. But it also remembers your shape, and it will greet you later—on the other side of the curtain, just before the story begins.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
